examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack - Bottom-Up Tabulation
examples.description: Standard dynamic programming approach using 2D table to find maximum profit
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Knapsack0_1(values, weights, n, W)
BEGIN
    // Create DP table with (n+1) rows and (W+1) columns
    DECLARE dp[0...n][0...W]
    
    // Base case: Initialize first row and first column to 0
    FOR w FROM 0 TO W DO
        dp[0][w] ← 0
    END FOR
    
    FOR i FROM 0 TO n DO
        dp[i][0] ← 0
    END FOR
    
    // Fill DP table
    FOR i FROM 1 TO n DO
        FOR w FROM 1 TO W DO
            IF weights[i] ≤ w THEN
                // Maximum of including or excluding item i
                dp[i][w] ← MAX(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weights[i]] + values[i])
            ELSE
                // Cannot include item i
                dp[i][w] ← dp[i-1][w]
            END IF
        END FOR
    END FOR
    
    RETURN dp[n][W]
END
examples.explanation: Time Complexity: O(n×W), Space Complexity: O(n×W). The table is filled bottom-up, considering each item and each possible weight capacity. The answer is stored in dp[n][W].

examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack - Space Optimized
examples.description: Optimized version using 1D array reducing space complexity to O(W)
examples.code:
def knapsack_0_1_space_optimized(values, weights, W):
    n = len(values)
    # Initialize 1D array with 0s
    dp = [0] * (W + 1)
    
    # Process each item
    for i in range(n):
        # Iterate backwards to ensure each item is used only once
        for w in range(W, weights[i] - 1, -1):
            # Either don't take item i or take item i
            dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w - weights[i]] + values[i])
    
    return dp[W]

# Example usage
values = [10, 15, 40, 60]
weights = [2, 3, 4, 5]
W = 8
print(knapsack_0_1_space_optimized(values, weights, W))  # Output: 70
examples.explanation: The key is iterating backward from W to weight[i]. This prevents using the same item multiple times in one iteration. Forward iteration would allow unbounded use of items.

examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack - Memoization (Top-Down)
examples.description: Recursive approach with memoization to avoid recomputing subproblems
examples.code:
def knapsack_memoization(values, weights, W, n, memo=None):
    # Initialize memo table if None
    if memo is None:
        memo = {}
    
    # Base case: no items or no capacity
    if n == 0 or W == 0:
        return 0
    
    # Return stored result if already computed
    if (n, W) in memo:
        return memo[(n, W)]
    
    # If weight of nth item is more than capacity, cannot include it
    if weights[n-1] > W:
        result = knapsack_memoization(values, weights, W, n-1, memo)
    else:
        # Maximum of including or excluding the item
        include = values[n-1] + knapsack_memoization(
            values, weights, W - weights[n-1], n-1, memo
        )
        exclude = knapsack_memoization(values, weights, W, n-1, memo)
        result = max(include, exclude)
    
    # Store result in memo table
    memo[(n, W)] = result
    return result

# Example usage
values = [10, 15, 40, 60]
weights = [2, 3, 4, 5]
W = 8
n = len(values)
print(knapsack_memoization(values, weights, W, n))  # Output: 70
examples.explanation: Uses dictionary for memoization. Time Complexity: O(n×W) - each unique (i,w) computed once. Space: O(n×W) for memo + O(n) for recursion stack.

examples.title: Reconstruct Selected Items
examples.description: Algorithm to find which items are selected after computing DP table
examples.code:
def find_selected_items(values, weights, W, n):
    # First compute the DP table
    dp = [[0] * (W + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
    
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for w in range(1, W + 1):
            if weights[i-1] <= w:
                dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weights[i-1]] + values[i-1])
            else:
                dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]
    
    # Now trace back to find selected items
    selected = []
    w = W
    
    for i in range(n, 0, -1):
        # If value came from excluding the item, it would be same as dp[i-1][w]
        if dp[i][w] != dp[i-1][w]:
            # This item was selected
            selected.append(i-1)  # Store index (0-based)
            w -= weights[i-1]
    
    selected.reverse()  # Reverse to get items in original order
    return selected, dp[n][W]

# Example usage
values = [10, 15, 40, 60]
weights = [2, 3, 4, 5]
W = 8
n = len(values)
items, max_profit = find_selected_items(values, weights, W, n)
print(f"Selected item indices: {items}")  # [1, 3] or [3, 1]
print(f"Maximum profit: {max_profit}")  # 70
examples.explanation: Start from dp[n][W] and compare with dp[n-1][w]. If different, item was selected. Subtract its weight and continue. Time: O(n+W) for reconstruction.
